danger_signals.visual_form: Sordid's mountain lair and tower structure
danger_signals.meaning: Primary antagonist stronghold; danger zone requiring preparation and magical items to breach

danger_signals.visual_form: Goblins in opening area near portal
danger_signals.meaning: Hostile creatures that threaten Simon immediately upon arrival; early danger indicator
Extreme environmental hazard; location of irreversible destruction (wand burning)
Weapon of mass threat; can turn people to stone; must be destroyed to defeat evil sorcerer

safety_signals.meaning: Safe haven; location of wizard mentorship and instruction

safety_signals.visual_form: Group of wizards gathered in village establishment
safety_signals.meaning: Allied faction providing instruction and legitimacy; Simon can gain wizard status through them
Non-hostile guide; helps Simon escape danger early on

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Village pub; Calypso's house; magical locations
Mountain lair; tower structure; Fiery Pits of Rondor connection

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fantasy setting with magic and monsters; parodies of Discworld, Lord of the Rings, fairy tales
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Simon transforms into wizard robes upon arrival; NPCs operate under magical logic and fantasy social structures

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sordid's lair remains accessible; threat is active and present
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs direct Simon toward rescue mission; wizards provide conditional aid (training in exchange for fees)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Portal opens to return Simon home; Fiery Pits consumed the wand
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Simon can leave the world; danger has been neutralized

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Simon awakens in bedroom; appears to be dream sequence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Assumption of safety; expectation of permanence

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Magical forces pull Simon back (true state)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Portal reopens; large gloved hand emerges
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Simon is reclaimed; indicates ongoing magical connection and unresolved destiny

environmental_storytelling.example: Chippy arrives as found dog with unreadable spellbook; book becomes key to plot; establishes magical intrusion into real world
Calypso's house vs. Sordid's mountain tower; domestic safety vs. ominous fortress; visual geography of conflict
Fantasy elements derived from Discworld, LOTR, Narnia, fairy tales; world communicates 'safe to be humorous' tone despite evil threat
Petrification wand; its existence and location (Sordid's tower, destroyable only in Fiery Pits) structures entire quest
Sordid's tower requires shrinking potion to breach; multi-stage design communicates escalating difficulty and puzzle-solving necessity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Flees from goblins; seeks refuge; follows Chippy for guidance
Actively seeks items; breaches defended locations; pursues Sordid
Leaves written instructions; presence felt through letter; guides Simon via proxy
Retreats to Fiery Pits; relies on petrification wand as last defense; ultimately pushes into flames (self-destruction or final defeat)
Require task completion and payment; transactional alliance; provide conditional support
Pursue Simon; require evasion; present early tutorial-level danger

notes: Simon the Sorcerer communicates world-state primarily through narrative and environment rather than explicit visual danger/safety systems. The game employs a comedic-fantasy tone (inspired by Discworld) which paradoxically softens danger signals while maintaining quest urgency. Faction identity is established through story position (allied wizards vs. evil sorcerer) rather than visual heraldry or color-coding. The parallel universe framework allows safe exploration of fantasy tropes and parodies. Portal mechanics (entry and exit) serve as visual bookends for world-state transitions. The ending's ambiguity (dream vs. reclamation) questions the permanence of world-state changes. As a point-and-click adventure, visual communication relies heavily on static environments, character positioning, and architectural contrast rather than dynamic behavioral systems.
